title: On the Face of It by Susan Hill
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Disability, loneliness, self-acceptance, empathy, and the importance of inner worth over outward appearance.

summary:
This play is about two people who feel isolated due to physical differences. Derry, a fourteen-year-old boy, has one side of his face burnt by acid when he was a baby. He is bitter and avoids people because they stare, pity him, or call him names. Mr. Lamb is an old man with a tin leg that was blown off in the war. He lives alone with a garden and keeps his gate open because he likes meeting people.

Derry climbs into Mr. Lamb’s garden thinking it is empty. Mr. Lamb does not react negatively to Derry’s face and talks to him kindly. He shares his philosophy: appearances do not matter and people are more important than how they look. He calls his garden a house of bees and finds beauty even in weeds. Though people call him Lamey-Lamb, he ignores them and claims he has many friends.

At first Derry is defensive. He says people call him a poor boy and even his mother avoids kissing his burnt side. Slowly, Mr. Lamb’s warmth changes him. Mr. Lamb says the same thing that separates people can also unite them. He urges Derry to stop hating and choose life.

Derry feels inspired and promises to return, despite his mother’s warning about strangers. While waiting, Mr. Lamb climbs a ladder for apples, falls, and dies. Derry comes back to find him on the ground and breaks down. The play shows how one meaningful human connection can transform a person’s outlook.

qa.q: Who is Mr Lamb? How does Derry get into his garden?
qa.a: Mr Lamb is an old man with a tin leg. He lives alone in a big house and keeps his garden open for everyone. Derry gets into his garden by climbing over the garden wall, even though the gate is open.

qa.q: What is it that draws Derry towards Mr Lamb in spite of himself?
qa.a: Derry is drawn towards Mr Lamb because Mr Lamb does not pity him or fear his burned face. He talks to Derry naturally and treats him like a normal person. Mr Lamb’s positive attitude, openness, and kindness make Derry feel accepted.

qa.q: In which section of the play does Mr Lamb display signs of loneliness and disappointment? What are the ways in which Mr Lamb tries to overcome these feelings?
qa.a: Mr Lamb shows loneliness and disappointment when he talks about people coming into his garden but not always staying. He tries to overcome loneliness by keeping his gate open, welcoming everyone, talking to people, listening, watching nature, making jelly and toffee, and staying active in his garden.

qa.q: The actual pain or inconvenience caused by a physical impairment is often much less than the sense of alienation felt by the person with disabilities. What is the kind of behaviour that the person expects from others?
qa.a: A person with disabilities expects normal, respectful, and sensitive behaviour from others. They do not want pity, fear, staring, avoidance, or false sympathy. They want acceptance, friendship, confidence, and equal treatment.

qa.q: Will Derry get back to his old seclusion or will Mr Lamb’s brief association effect a change in the kind of life he will lead in the future?
qa.a: Mr Lamb’s brief association will probably change Derry’s life. Derry returns to the garden against his mother’s warning, which shows that he has already started changing. He now wants the world and wants to live more openly instead of hiding himself.
